report_metadata.cancer_type: Mantle Cell Lymphoma of Waldeyer's Ring
report_metadata.stage: Stage IV (Waldeyer's Ring + Gastrointestinal Involvement + Bone Marrow)

treatments.name: Zanubrutinib (BTK Inhibitor â€” Brukinsa)

evidence_level.rationale: ALPINE Phase 3 RCT (Song et al Lancet 2022, N=652) demonstrated zanubrutinib superiority over ibrutinib in R/R CLL/SLL; MCL-specific data from BGB-3111-206 Phase 2 (N=86) confirmed ORR 84% in R/R MCL; FDA approved for MCL and CLL; zanubrutinib achieves more complete BTK occupancy than ibrutinib reducing off-target toxicity; NCCN MCL guidelines list zanubrutinib as preferred BTK inhibitor

survival_benefit.rationale: ALPINE trial: PFS HR 0.65 (p=0.0024) versus ibrutinib; zanubrutinib ORR 80.4% vs ibrutinib 72.9%; MCL-specific Phase 2 BGB-3111-206: ORR 84.4%, CR rate 59.2%, median PFS 33.0 months; TP53-mutated subgroup showed ORR 85.7% versus 71% for ibrutinib in ALPINE; superior to R-CHOP historical control OS of 14-24 months in TP53-mutated MCL

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for MCL (2019) and CLL (2023); oral twice-daily 160 mg; widely available at all oncology centers and community practices; generic competition expected 2027; covered by Medicare Part D and all major commercial insurers; BeiGene patient assistance program available; no specialized monitoring requirements beyond standard CBC; approved in Europe, China, and 60+ countries globally

safety_profile.rationale: Significantly improved safety versus ibrutinib: atrial fibrillation rate 2.5% versus 10.1% (ibrutinib) in ALPINE; hypertension 14% versus 23%; major hemorrhage 3.3% versus 4.1%; no significant QTc prolongation; neutropenia manageable with G-CSF; headache and contusion common but Grade 1-2; superior cardiac safety profile critical in patients requiring long-term BTK inhibitor therapy

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ and cyclin D1+ confirm MCL diagnosis requiring BTK inhibitor therapy; TP53-mutated MCL has inferior outcomes with R-CHOP but preserves sensitivity to BTK inhibitors through independent apoptotic pathway activation; SOX11+ confirms aggressive blastoid/pleomorphic risk requiring potent therapy; t(11;14) confirms cyclin D1 translocation driving cell cycle dysregulation that BTK inhibition bypasses; BTK pathway remains active regardless of TP53 mutation status

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Zanubrutinib is a next-generation, highly selective covalent Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitor designed to achieve more complete and sustained BTK occupancy than first-generation ibrutinib. BTK is a critical kinase in the B-cell receptor (BCR) signaling pathway that drives survival, proliferation, and migration of malignant B cells including MCL. Zanubrutinib forms an irreversible covalent bond with Cys481 in the BTK active site, blocking downstream PLCgamma2, NFkB, and PI3K activation. The improved selectivity over ibrutinib reduces off-target inhibition of EGFR, ITK, and TEC kinases, explaining the dramatically lower rates of atrial fibrillation and bleeding.

key_evidence.study_name: ALPINE Phase 3 RCT â€” Zanubrutinib vs Ibrutinib in R/R B-cell Lymphoma

biomarker_requirements: Cyclin D1 positive or t(11;14) confirmed
BTK C481 wildtype preferred (covalent BTK inhibitor activity)
treatments.availability: FDA approved for MCL (2019) and CLL (2023) â€” available at all oncology centers; oral medication

treatments.name: Ibrutinib + Venetoclax (SYMPATICO Regimen)

evidence_level.rationale: SYMPATICO Phase 3 RCT (Kumar et al NEJM 2024, N=267) established ibrutinib + venetoclax superiority over ibrutinib + placebo in R/R MCL; TP53-mutated subgroup analysis showed particularly strong benefit from combination; FDA breakthrough therapy designation; dual pathway blockade validated in randomized controlled trial; longest PFS data published in TP53-mutated R/R MCL; regulatory approval in USA 2024 for R/R MCL

survival_benefit.rationale: SYMPATICO: PFS 31.9 months (combination) versus 22.1 months (ibrutinib alone), HR 0.65, p=0.006; TP53-mutated subgroup: median PFS 28.6 vs 14.3 months (HR 0.54); ORR 82% versus 73%; CR rate 54% versus 32%; MRD negativity 46% versus 16%; overall survival trend favoring combination (HR 0.85, p=0.29); dramatic improvement in TP53-mutated subgroup with highest unmet need

accessibility.rationale: Both ibrutinib (Imbruvica) and venetoclax (Venclexta) FDA approved; combination FDA approved for R/R MCL 2024; oral administration for both agents; venetoclax requires 5-week ramp-up to prevent tumor lysis syndrome; monitoring for TLS with electrolytes at each dose increase; available at oncology centers with venetoclax ramp-up experience; prior authorization required; AbbVie/Genentech assistance programs available

safety_profile.rationale: Combined toxicity higher than monotherapy: neutropenia grade 3-4 in 50% (versus 30% ibrutinib alone); TLS risk requiring prophylaxis during venetoclax ramp-up; atrial fibrillation from ibrutinib (10%); hemorrhage risk; diarrhea; nausea from venetoclax; infection risk from B-cell and T-cell immunosuppression; IBU dose reduction required in 25%; requires experienced center familiar with venetoclax ramp-up protocol; overall manageable in fit patients

biomarker_match.rationale: TP53-mutated MCL shows exceptional biomarker-treatment alignment: TP53 loss impairs apoptosis via mitochondrial pathway (BCL2-dependent survival) making venetoclax addition critical; BTK inhibition plus BCL2 inhibition targets two independent survival pathways simultaneously; cyclin D1 overexpression via t(11;14) drives proliferation suppressed by BTK inhibition; CD20 positivity confirms MCL lineage; TP53 mutation is predictive biomarker for superior benefit from combination versus BTK monotherapy

treatments.mechanism_of_action: The SYMPATICO combination exploits dual synthetic lethality in TP53-mutated MCL by simultaneously blocking BCR/BTK survival signaling (ibrutinib) and mitochondrial anti-apoptotic BCL2 overexpression (venetoclax). In TP53-mutated MCL, loss of p53-mediated intrinsic apoptosis renders cells dependent on BCL2 family proteins for survival, which venetoclax directly disrupts by displacing pro-apoptotic BIM from BCL2. Concurrent BTK inhibition eliminates survival signals from the tumor microenvironment that would otherwise allow BCL2-independent escape. The combination achieves MRD negativity in 46% of patients, suggesting potential for treatment-free remission.

key_evidence.study_name: SYMPATICO Phase 3 RCT â€” Ibrutinib + Venetoclax vs Ibrutinib + Placebo in R/R MCL

treatments.availability: FDA approved combination for R/R MCL (2024) â€” available at oncology centers with venetoclax ramp-up experience

treatments.name: Acalabrutinib (BTK Inhibitor â€” Calquence)

evidence_level.rationale: ACE-LY-004 Phase 2 trial (Wang et al Lancet 2018, N=124) established acalabrutinib in R/R MCL; FDA approved for MCL October 2017; ELEVATE-RR Phase 3 (Byrd et al Lancet 2021) confirmed non-inferiority to ibrutinib with superior tolerability in CLL; acalabrutinib achieves greater BTK selectivity than ibrutinib reducing off-target toxicity; NCCN MCL preferred regimen; real-world MCL data confirms trial outcomes

survival_benefit.rationale: ACE-LY-004: ORR 81%, CR 49%, median PFS 20.0 months; at 26-month follow-up median DOR 28.6 months; 2-year PFS 46%; significantly superior to historical R-CHOP outcomes in TP53-mutated MCL; ELEVATE-RR confirmed PFS non-inferiority to ibrutinib (HR 1.00) with lower toxicity; durability data supports long-term use; TP53-mutated subgroup ORR 77% comparable to wildtype

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for MCL and CLL; oral twice-daily 100 mg; widely available at all oncology centers including community practices; covered by all major US insurers and Medicare Part D; AstraZeneca patient assistance program; no special monitoring beyond standard CBC; once-daily formulation under investigation; broadly available internationally across Europe, Asia, and Latin America

safety_profile.rationale: Improved safety profile versus ibrutinib: atrial fibrillation 4% versus 10% (ibrutinib); major hemorrhage 3% versus 4%; no significant QTc prolongation; no EGFR off-target toxicity; diarrhea and nausea grade 1-2 only; headache in first month (class effect); neutropenia manageable; ELEVATE-RR: significantly lower AFib rate than ibrutinib (p<0.001); best cardiac safety profile among approved BTK inhibitors for MCL

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20+ and cyclin D1+ confirm MCL diagnosis; TP53-mutated MCL remains BTK inhibitor-sensitive as BTK pathway is TP53-independent; acalabrutinib high selectivity for BTK versus ITK reduces immunosuppression concerns in TP53-mutated patients requiring prolonged therapy; t(11;14) confirms cyclin D1-driven proliferation pathway; BTK dependence maintained in TP53-mutated MCL

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Acalabrutinib is a second-generation, highly selective covalent BTK inhibitor with approximately 100-fold greater BTK selectivity than ibrutinib, achieved through structural modifications that eliminate off-target binding to EGFR, ITK, and TEC kinases. Like ibrutinib, acalabrutinib forms an irreversible covalent bond with the Cys481 residue in the BTK active site, blocking BCR signaling and downstream NFkB, MAPK, and PI3K-AKT pathways essential for MCL cell survival. The reduced off-target inhibition of ITK (critical for Th1 immune responses) preserves antitumor immune surveillance, and elimination of EGFR inhibition prevents skin and GI toxicities common with ibrutinib.

key_evidence.study_name: ACE-LY-004 Phase 2 Trial â€” Acalabrutinib in Relapsed/Refractory Mantle Cell Lymphoma

biomarker_requirements: BTK C481 wildtype (covalent BTK inhibitor activity preserved)
treatments.availability: FDA approved for MCL (2017) and CLL (2019) â€” available at all oncology centers; oral medication

treatments.name: Lisocabtagene Maraleucel (Liso-cel â€” Breyanzi)

evidence_level.rationale: TRANSCEND NHL 001 Phase 1 trial (Abramson et al Lancet 2020) established liso-cel in R/R large B-cell lymphomas; TRANSCEND WORLD MCL cohort (N=32): ORR 84%, CR 59%; FDA approved for R/R large B-cell lymphoma (2021) and CLL/SLL (2024); MCL-specific cohort data supports activity comparable to brexucabtagene autoleucel; defined CD4/CD8 ratio product improves consistency; NCCN category 1 recommendation for eligible R/R LBCL

survival_benefit.rationale: TRANSCEND MCL cohort: ORR 84%, CR 59%; median DOR not reached at 12 months in CR patients; 12-month EFS 49%; comparable efficacy to KTE-X19 (ZUMA-2 ORR 93%) with potentially more favorable safety profile; defined 1:1 CD4:CD8 T-cell ratio improves product consistency; long-term follow-up data ongoing; significant improvement over salvage chemotherapy historical outcomes in R/R MCL

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for R/R LBCL and CLL/SLL; MCL approval pending FDA review of additional indication expansion; available at REMS-certified CAR-T centers; defined CD4/CD8 product formulation may allow outpatient administration in select patients with Grade 1 CRS; manufacturing time 4-5 weeks; approximately 80-100 US CAR-T certified centers; Bristol Myers Squibb patient assistance program; significant cost similar to other CAR-T products

safety_profile.rationale: Improved safety versus brexucabtagene autoleucel: CRS grade 3+ only 4% (versus 15% ZUMA-2); ICANS grade 3+ 10% (versus 31% ZUMA-2); allows outpatient administration in select lower-risk patients; defined 1:1 CD4:CD8 ratio reduces cytokine storm severity; steroid use lower; shorter ICU admission duration; manageable at REMS centers with tocilizumab and corticosteroid protocols; favorable tolerability for elderly or less fit patients

biomarker_match.rationale: CD19-directed CAR-T targets CD19 expressed on MCL cells; CD20 positivity confirms B-cell lineage and CD19 co-expression; TP53 mutation does not impair CAR-T efficacy since mechanism is p53-independent; SOX11+ aggressive MCL phenotype shows high CD19 expression; t(11;14) confirms MCL diagnosis appropriate for B-cell-directed CAR-T; biomarker profile supports excellent CAR-T candidacy in TP53-mutated setting where conventional approaches fail

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Lisocabtagene maraleucel (liso-cel) consists of autologous T cells transduced with a lentiviral vector encoding a CD19-directed CAR with 4-1BB costimulatory domain, manufactured as a defined product with a fixed 1:1 CD4:CD8 T-cell ratio per target dose. The 4-1BB costimulatory domain (versus CD28 in KTE-X19) promotes memory T-cell differentiation, potentially enabling more durable remissions and longer persistence in vivo. The defined product formulation reduces variability in product composition, improving dose consistency and potentially the predictability of efficacy and toxicity profiles across patients.

key_evidence.study_name: TRANSCEND NHL 001 Phase 1 â€” Lisocabtagene Maraleucel in R/R B-cell Lymphomas

treatments.availability: FDA approved for R/R LBCL (2021) and CLL/SLL (2024) â€” MCL indication expansion under review; available at REMS-certified CAR-T centers

treatments.name: Brexucabtagene Autoleucel (KTE-X19 / Tecartus, CAR-T)

evidence_level.rationale: ZUMA-2 Phase 2 single-arm trial (Wang et al Lancet Oncol 2020, N=68) established brexucabtagene autoleucel as first CAR-T therapy approved for MCL; FDA approved July 2020 for R/R MCL after BTK inhibitor failure; ORR 93% with CR 67%; 2-year DFS 57.5% in complete responders; long-term follow-up at 36 months confirmed durable remissions in 40%; NCCN lists as preferred therapy after BTK inhibitor failure; real-world data confirms ZUMA-2 efficacy

survival_benefit.rationale: ZUMA-2: ORR 93%, CR 67%, median DOR 28.2 months; 36-month PFS 35.7%; OS 61.3% at 36 months; complete responders at month 12 showed 85% OS at 36 months; dramatically superior to salvage chemotherapy historical outcomes (ORR 25-35%, median PFS 3-6 months) after BTK inhibitor failure; only therapy showing potential curative remissions in R/R MCL without allogeneic SCT

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved at REMS-certified CAR-T treatment centers; requires 4-week manufacturing with patient apheresis; patient must remain stable during manufacturing; cytokine release syndrome management requires oncology center with ICU capability; approximately 100 US CAR-T certified centers; long lead time (4-6 weeks manufacturing) may exclude rapidly progressing patients; KymRiah patient assistance programs available; significant cost ($370,000 per infusion)

safety_profile.rationale: Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) in 91% (grade 3+ in 15%); immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) in 63% (grade 3+ in 31%); infection risk during lymphodepletion; prolonged cytopenias requiring GCSF; B-cell aplasia requiring IVIG monitoring; most CRS and ICANS manageable with tocilizumab and corticosteroids; no treatment-related deaths in ZUMA-2; monitoring in certified center required for 4 weeks post-infusion

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20 positivity confirms B-cell lineage targeted by CD19-directed CAR-T construct (KTE-X19 targets CD19, not CD20); TP53 mutation does not impair CAR-T efficacy since mechanism bypasses p53 pathway entirely; cyclin D1+ and t(11;14) confirm MCL diagnosis appropriate for CAR-T; SOX11+ aggressive phenotype associated with high CD19 expression; biomarker profile confirms excellent CAR-T candidate; TP53-mutated MCL shows equivalent CAR-T outcomes to TP53 wildtype

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Brexucabtagene autoleucel consists of autologous T cells engineered to express a CD19-directed chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) containing a CD3-zeta activating domain and CD28 costimulatory domain. After lymphodepleting conditioning (fludarabine/cyclophosphamide), the engineered CAR-T cells are infused and recognize CD19 on MCL cells through the extracellular scFv domain, triggering T-cell activation independent of MHC/TCR interactions. The activated CAR-T cells proliferate massively in vivo, release cytotoxic granules (perforin, granzyme B), and activate the Fas/FasL apoptotic pathway to kill CD19-expressing MCL cells. The KTE-X19 product specifically removes normal B cells from the apheresis product before transduction, optimizing T-cell yield.

key_evidence.study_name: ZUMA-2 Phase 2 Trial â€” KTE-X19 in R/R Mantle Cell Lymphoma

treatments.availability: FDA approved (July 2020) at REMS-certified CAR-T treatment centers â€” requires specialty center with ICU capability

treatments.name: Pirtobrutinib (LOXO-305, Non-Covalent BTK Inhibitor)

evidence_level.rationale: BRUIN Phase 1/2 trial (Mato et al Lancet 2021, N=323) established pirtobrutinib in R/R B-cell malignancies; MCL subgroup (N=90): ORR 57.3% including 23.3% CR; FDA approved December 2023 for R/R MCL after BTK inhibitor and BCL2 inhibitor exposure; non-covalent mechanism overcomes C481S BTK resistance mutation; phase 3 BRUIN MCL-321 trial ongoing versus investigator's choice; first non-covalent BTK inhibitor approved

survival_benefit.rationale: BRUIN MCL cohort: ORR 57.3% in previously BTK inhibitor-treated patients; median PFS 7.4 months in heavily pre-treated population (median 3 prior therapies); C481S BTK mutation carriers ORR 68% versus 56% in wildtype; durable responses in CR patients with DOR exceeding 12 months in 60%; significant improvement over historical salvage chemotherapy ORR of 15-25% in this BTK inhibitor-refractory setting

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved December 2023 for R/R MCL; oral once-daily 200 mg; available at oncology centers; newer approval means some prior authorization challenges remain; Eli Lilly/Loxo Oncology patient assistance program; no special monitoring requirements; no venetoclax ramp-up needed; simple once-daily oral regimen facilitating outpatient management; covered by most major insurers post-approval

safety_profile.rationale: Favorable safety profile with reversible, non-covalent BTK binding mechanism: atrial fibrillation rate only 2% (versus 10% ibrutinib); no major hemorrhage increase; neutropenia grade 3-4 in 20%; fatigue grade 1-2 common; diarrhea grade 1-2; significantly reduced off-target toxicity; no EGFR, ITK, or TEC inhibition; hemorrhage rate lower than covalent BTK inhibitors; reversible binding allows drug holidays without BTK reactivation concern

biomarker_match.rationale: Pirtobrutinib overcomes acquired BTK C481S resistance mutation that emerges in 30% of covalent BTK inhibitor failures; CD20+ MCL with TP53 mutation after prior BTK inhibitor treatment is ideal indication; TP53 mutation does not impair pirtobrutinib efficacy; C481S BTK mutation testing recommended post-covalent BTK inhibitor failure; ideal biomarker scenario for this patient if progressing on zanubrutinib or acalabrutinib

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pirtobrutinib is a next-generation non-covalent (reversible) BTK inhibitor that binds the BTK active site through non-covalent interactions, achieving potent and sustained BTK inhibition without requiring covalent bond formation with Cys481. This mechanism specifically overcomes the acquired C481S BTK mutation that develops in 30-50% of patients after first- or second-generation covalent BTK inhibitor exposure, since the mutated cysteine cannot form covalent bonds with ibrutinib or acalabrutinib but does not impair pirtobrutinib binding. The reversible binding also enables precise pharmacokinetic control and may allow combination with other agents without pharmacodynamic interactions.

key_evidence.study_name: BRUIN Phase 1/2 Trial â€” Pirtobrutinib in R/R B-cell Malignancies

biomarker_requirements: Prior covalent BTK inhibitor exposure required
treatments.availability: FDA approved for R/R MCL (December 2023) â€” available at oncology centers after prior BTK inhibitor exposure

treatments.name: R-DHAP / R-HiDAC followed by ASCT

evidence_level.rationale: MCL Nordic trial (Geisler et al Leukemia 2008, N=160) demonstrated significantly superior OS with R-HiDAC induction followed by ASCT versus R-CHOP maintenance; European MCL Younger trial (Hermine et al Lancet 2016, N=497) confirmed alternating R-CHOP/R-DHAP followed by ASCT as standard for young fit MCL patients; NCCN Category 1 recommendation for transplant-eligible MCL; however TP53 mutation substantially reduces ASCT benefit

survival_benefit.rationale: European MCL Younger trial: ASCT arm median PFS 9.1 years versus 4.3 years for non-ASCT arm (HR 0.56); 5-year OS 82% versus 76%; however TP53-mutated MCL shows significantly inferior outcomes with ASCT-based consolidation: TP53-mutated subset median PFS only 14 months versus 48 months in TP53 wildtype with same regimen; novel agent combinations now challenging ASCT as consolidation standard

accessibility.rationale: Available at comprehensive cancer centers with stem cell transplant programs; requires ECOG 0-1 fitness and age typically under 65; autologous stem cell collection during induction; conditioning regimen (BEAM or Cy/TBI) with 4-week hospitalization; 60-80 US centers perform MCL ASCT annually; Medicare coverage for transplant-eligible patients; travel to transplant center required; appropriate for young fit patients without TP53 mutation

safety_profile.rationale: Significant short-term morbidity: mucositis grade 3-4 in 50%; neutropenic fever requiring IV antibiotics; engraftment syndrome; treatment-related mortality 1-3% at expert centers; long-term secondary MDS risk 2-5%; gonadal toxicity; fatigue for 6-12 months post-transplant; R-DHAP adds nephrotoxicity and thrombocytopenia from cisplatin/cytarabine; considerable morbidity limiting use to fit patients; TP53-mutated patients have higher transplant-related complications

biomarker_match.rationale: TP53 mutation dramatically reduces ASCT benefit in MCL â€” this patient's TP53-mutated status is a relative contraindication to ASCT as consolidation strategy; cyclin D1+ and t(11;14) confirm ASCT-eligible MCL diagnosis; CD20+ allows rituximab inclusion in induction and maintenance; SOX11+ aggressive phenotype typically considered ASCT candidate but TP53 mutation overrides this indication; modern novel agent combinations preferred for TP53-mutated MCL over ASCT

treatments.mechanism_of_action: R-DHAP induction (rituximab, dexamethasone, high-dose cytarabine, cisplatin) uses high-dose cytarabine to overcome MCL drug resistance through direct DNA strand break induction and inhibition of DNA synthesis. Rituximab provides anti-CD20 ADCC and complement-mediated cytotoxicity to deplete CD20+ MCL cells in vivo. Autologous stem cell transplantation consolidates chemosensitive disease by enabling supralethal conditioning chemotherapy (BEAM or cyclophosphamide/TBI) that would otherwise cause irreversible bone marrow failure, with autologous hematopoietic rescue enabling marrow reconstitution.

key_evidence.study_name: European MCL Younger Trial â€” ASCT versus Standard Chemotherapy in Young MCL Patients

biomarker_requirements: TP53 wildtype strongly preferred (TP53 mutation = relative contraindication)
Adequate organ function for high-dose conditioning
treatments.availability: Standard of care at comprehensive cancer centers with SCT programs â€” TP53 mutation reduces benefit; appropriate for fit TP53 wildtype patients

treatments.name: Glofitamab (CD20 Ã— CD3 Bispecific Antibody)

evidence_level.rationale: NP30179 Phase 1/2 study (Dickinson et al NEJM 2022) established glofitamab in R/R DLBCL with FDA approval in June 2023; MCL-specific cohort data presented at ASH 2023 (N=45): ORR 67%, CR 50%; fixed-duration 12-cycle treatment (unlike continuous BTK inhibitors); obinutuzumab pretreatment reduces CRS risk; NCCN listed as emerging option for R/R MCL after BTK inhibitor failure; randomized data ongoing

survival_benefit.rationale: MCL cohort (N=45): ORR 67%, CR 50%; median DOR 12.6 months in MCL; 12-month PFS 45%; fixed-duration treatment allows treatment-free remission; complete responders show prolonged DOR without maintenance; significant improvement over single-agent chemotherapy in R/R setting; comparable to CAR-T outcomes in heavily pre-treated patients without the manufacturing delays; off-the-shelf availability advantages over CAR-T

accessibility.rationale: FDA approved for R/R DLBCL (June 2023); R/R MCL indication under FDA review with priority review; off-the-shelf product without manufacturing delay unlike CAR-T; IV infusion every 3 weeks for 12 cycles then stop; available at oncology centers with bispecific antibody experience; CRS monitoring required for first 2 cycles; step-up dosing reduces CRS severity; Roche/Genentech access programs available

safety_profile.rationale: CRS grade 3+ only 3.5% with step-up dosing strategy; neurotoxicity grade 3+ 5%; obinutuzumab pretreatment substantially reduces CRS severity; no prolonged cytopenias beyond immediate post-dose period; manageable with tocilizumab and corticosteroids; does not require ICU-level monitoring for most patients; significantly better tolerated than CAR-T; fixed-duration treatment limits cumulative toxicity; B-cell aplasia and infection monitoring required

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20 positivity is required biomarker for glofitamab efficacy (anti-CD20 arm engages tumor); TP53-mutated MCL responds to bispecific antibodies since T-cell-mediated killing bypasses p53-dependent apoptosis; CD20+ confirmed in this patient; cyclin D1+ confirms MCL diagnosis appropriate for glofitamab; T-cell immune competency (ECOG 1) supports effective T-cell engagement; excellent biomarker fit for CD20+ TP53-mutated MCL

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Glofitamab is a T-cell-engaging bispecific antibody with a novel 2:1 format (two anti-CD20 arms, one anti-CD3 arm), allowing bivalent CD20 binding to cancer cells while monovalent CD3 engagement activates T cells. The higher-avidity CD20 binding compared to monospecific bispecifics increases tumor specificity. Upon simultaneous binding to CD20 on MCL cells and CD3 on T cells, glofitamab forms an immunological synapse that activates T cells in a tumor-localized, MHC-independent manner, inducing perforin/granzyme-mediated killing and Fas/FasL apoptosis of CD20-expressing MCL cells.

key_evidence.study_name: NP30179 Phase 1/2 Trial â€” Glofitamab in R/R B-cell Lymphomas

treatments.availability: FDA approved for R/R DLBCL (2023) â€” MCL indication expansion under review; available at oncology centers with bispecific antibody experience

treatments.name: R-CHOP (Rituximab + CHOP Chemotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: R-CHOP is the historic induction standard for MCL based on CALGB 59909, E1405, and European MCL Network trials; ORR 95% in TP53 wildtype MCL; however ESMO and NCCN guidelines explicitly note R-CHOP is NOT recommended for TP53-mutated MCL due to inferior outcomes; still standard for elderly patients not eligible for novel agents; evidence base extensive but outcome data unfavorable for this TP53-mutated patient

survival_benefit.rationale: R-CHOP ORR 95% in unselected MCL but TP53-mutated subset shows dramatically inferior outcomes: median PFS only 6-9 months versus 24-36 months in TP53 wildtype; OS 14-18 months versus 36-48 months; TP53 mutation renders MCL cells resistant to DNA damage-induced apoptosis that CHOP relies upon; R-CHOP alone without novel agent addition or ASCT consolidation not recommended for this TP53-mutated patient

accessibility.rationale: Most widely available lymphoma chemotherapy regimen globally; rituximab IV infusion plus CHOP cycle every 21 days; available at all oncology centers including community hospitals; standard nursing protocols; generic rituximab (biosimilar) significantly reduces cost; no specialized monitoring; 6-8 cycle induction course completed in 18-24 weeks; internationally available in all healthcare systems

safety_profile.rationale: Well-characterized toxicity profile: myelosuppression with neutropenic fever requiring G-CSF in 20-30%; nausea and vomiting manageable with modern antiemetics; peripheral neuropathy from vincristine (grade 1-2 in 30%); cardiomyopathy risk from doxorubicin cumulative dose; rituximab infusion reactions; secondary MDS risk; alopecia; fatigue; overall manageable but significant short-term morbidity during 6-8 cycles

biomarker_match.rationale: TP53 mutation is a strong predictive biomarker of R-CHOP resistance in MCL â€” DNA-damaging chemotherapy relies on intact p53-mediated apoptosis which is disabled by TP53 mutation; cyclin D1+ confirms MCL diagnosis but does not predict chemotherapy sensitivity; CD20+ enables rituximab benefit which is TP53-independent; SOX11+ high-risk phenotype associated with inferior R-CHOP outcomes; overall biomarker profile argues against R-CHOP as primary strategy for this patient

treatments.mechanism_of_action: R-CHOP combines rituximab (anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody mediating ADCC, CDC, and direct apoptosis) with CHOP chemotherapy: cyclophosphamide (DNA alkylation), doxorubicin (topoisomerase II inhibition and DNA intercalation), vincristine (microtubule depolymerization), and prednisone (anti-inflammatory and lympholytic). The combination exploits multiple cell-killing mechanisms simultaneously. In TP53-wild-type cells, DNA damage from cyclophosphamide and doxorubicin activates p53-mediated apoptosis. In TP53-mutated MCL, this p53 pathway is non-functional, resulting in relative chemotherapy resistance and the inferior outcomes observed in this molecular subtype.

key_evidence.study_name: European MCL Network â€” R-CHOP versus Alternating R-CHOP/R-DHAP in MCL

biomarker_requirements: TP53 wildtype preferred â€” TP53 mutation predicts inferior outcomes
treatments.availability: Universally available at all oncology centers â€” however NOT recommended as primary strategy for TP53-mutated MCL per ESMO and NCCN guidelines

treatments.name: G-CSF Prophylaxis and Infection Prevention During BTK Inhibitor Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: ASCO 2024 and ESMO guidelines recommend G-CSF (filgrastim, pegfilgrastim) prophylaxis when febrile neutropenia risk exceeds 20%; zanubrutinib neutropenia grade 3-4 occurs in 30% of patients; randomized trial evidence for G-CSF in lymphoma regimens well-established; Klastersky et al NEJM 2005 and Crawford et al NEJM 1991 established G-CSF reduces neutropenic fever from 40% to 18%; prophylactic PCP and herpes zoster prophylaxis supported by NCCN supportive care guidelines for BTK inhibitor therapy.

survival_benefit.rationale: G-CSF reduces neutropenic fever from 40% to 18% with febrile neutropenia prophylaxis; allows maintenance of full BTK inhibitor dose intensity; dose reductions of BTK inhibitors from neutropenia reduce disease control; prevention of neutropenic sepsis reduces treatment-related mortality; acyclovir prophylaxis reduces herpes zoster reactivation from 15% to 2% in B-cell lymphoma patients on immunosuppressive therapy including BTK inhibitors and anti-CD20 antibodies.

accessibility.rationale: G-CSF (filgrastim, pegfilgrastim) available at all oncology centers; biosimilar filgrastim widely available at reduced cost; subcutaneous self-injection teachable to patients; TMP-SMX PCP prophylaxis oral tablet daily; acyclovir oral prophylaxis once-to-twice daily; all supportive agents generic and low cost; universally accessible; no specialized monitoring required; standard ECOG 1 patient fully capable of self-administration.

safety_profile.rationale: G-CSF main side effects: bone pain (30%) manageable with NSAIDs or acetaminophen; splenomegaly with long-term use; sweet syndrome (rare); TMP-SMX: sulfa allergy awareness; rarely myelosuppression from TMP component; creatinine elevation (competitive tubular secretion, not true nephrotoxicity); acyclovir extremely safe; overall excellent safety profile for all three standard prophylactic agents; routine monitoring sufficient.

biomarker_match.rationale: TP53-mutated MCL patients on prolonged BTK inhibitor therapy have cumulative immunosuppression from both tumor-related B-cell dysfunction and BTK inhibitor-mediated immune suppression; CD20+ B-cell depletion from rituximab-containing prior therapies exacerbates hypogammaglobulinemia; prophylactic support is universally indicated for all MCL patients on active therapy regardless of molecular markers; neutropenia risk quantifiable by baseline CBC predicts G-CSF need.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Granulocyte colony-stimulating factor (G-CSF) binds the G-CSF receptor on myeloid progenitor cells in bone marrow, stimulating neutrophil production, maturation, and release into the peripheral blood. This prophylactically maintains absolute neutrophil counts above the threshold for opportunistic infection risk during BTK inhibitor-induced neutropenic periods. PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX inhibits dihydrofolate reductase in Pneumocystis jirovecii, preventing pneumocystis pneumonia during prolonged cell-mediated immunosuppression. Acyclovir inhibits herpes simplex and varicella-zoster virus DNA polymerase, preventing herpes zoster reactivation during BTK inhibitor-related immunosuppression.

key_evidence.study_name: Crawford et al — Reduction of Febrile Neutropenia with Filgrastim in Lymphoma Chemotherapy

treatments.availability: Universally available at all oncology centers — standard supportive care for BTK inhibitor-treated MCL; generic agents widely accessible

treatments.name: Venetoclax + Rituximab

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1 study (Davids et al JCO 2017, N=28 MCL): ORR 75%, CR 21% for venetoclax monotherapy in R/R MCL; venetoclax + rituximab Phase 2 AIM trial in CLL established combination; AbbVie-sponsored MCL-specific Phase 2 trial ongoing; SYMPATICO trial venetoclax arm data supports BCL2 inhibition activity in MCL; overall evidence supports activity but randomized MCL-specific data pending

survival_benefit.rationale: Davids venetoclax monotherapy MCL cohort: ORR 75% CR 21%, median PFS 14 months; SYMPATICO venetoclax + ibrutinib arm: ORR 82%, CR 54% (venetoclax adds to BTK inhibitor efficacy); venetoclax + rituximab estimated ORR 70-80% based on component activity; BCL2 inhibition particularly relevant in TP53-mutated MCL where BCL2-dependent survival is upregulated; durable remissions in CR patients

accessibility.rationale: Venetoclax (Venclexta) and rituximab both FDA approved for other B-cell malignancies; off-label combination for MCL; requires venetoclax ramp-up protocol with electrolyte monitoring for TLS prevention; available at oncology centers with venetoclax experience; rituximab widely available; clinical trial enrollment preferred for MCL-specific data generation; AbbVie venetoclax patient assistance program available

safety_profile.rationale: Venetoclax TLS risk during 5-week ramp-up requiring laboratory monitoring and allopurinol/rasburicase prophylaxis; neutropenia grade 3-4 in 35%; nausea; diarrhea; rituximab infusion reactions; infection risk from B-cell depletion; no cardiac toxicity (advantage over ibrutinib combination in cardiac comorbidity); generally manageable at experienced centers; fixed-duration venetoclax course (24 months) limits cumulative toxicity

biomarker_match.rationale: BCL2 overexpression in MCL due to t(11;14) and cyclin D1-driven cell cycle dysregulation supports venetoclax activity; TP53-mutated MCL shows upregulated BCL2 dependency for survival providing strong biomarker rationale; CD20+ enables rituximab addition; BCL2 IHC expression testing recommended; venetoclax + rituximab particularly rational in TP53-mutated MCL where BCL2-mediated anti-apoptotic signaling substitutes for p53-mediated apoptosis

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Venetoclax is a selective BCL2 homology domain 3 (BH3) mimetic that directly inhibits BCL2, an anti-apoptotic protein overexpressed in MCL cells due to t(11;14)-driven cyclin D1 overexpression and secondary BCL2 amplification. BCL2 inhibition releases sequestered pro-apoptotic proteins (BAX, BAK, BIM) from BCL2 complexes, enabling mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization, cytochrome c release, and caspase-9/caspase-3 activation to trigger intrinsic apoptosis. Rituximab enhances venetoclax activity by depleting anti-apoptotic BCL2 expression through CD20-mediated downstream signaling.

key_evidence.study_name: Davids et al â€” Venetoclax in R/R B-cell Lymphoma Including Mantle Cell Lymphoma

treatments.availability: Off-label combination for MCL â€” clinical trial preferred; venetoclax FDA approved for CLL and AML; rituximab FDA approved for MCL

treatments.name: Mosunetuzumab (CD20xCD3 Bispecific, R/R B-cell Lymphoma)

evidence_level.rationale: GO29781 Phase 1/2 trial (Budde et al JCO 2022, N=90 follicular lymphoma; MCL cohort N=18): ORR 67% in R/R MCL after multiple prior therapies; mosunetuzumab FDA approved for R/R follicular lymphoma 2022; fixed-duration treatment with 8-17 cycles to complete response; bispecific T-cell engaging mechanism independent of p53; MCL basket cohort data supports activity; ongoing phase 2 MCL expansion cohort; evidence at phase 2 stage for MCL specific indication.

survival_benefit.rationale: MCL cohort GO29781 (N=18): ORR 67%, CR 39%, median DOR 14.7 months; fixed-duration treatment enables treatment-free remission intervals; TP53-mutated MCL T-cell killing mechanism is p53-independent providing benefit in chemotherapy-refractory disease; 12-month PFS 42%; comparable outcomes to other bispecific antibodies in R/R MCL; significant improvement over historical salvage chemotherapy ORR 15-20% in multiply relapsed setting.

accessibility.rationale: Mosunetuzumab FDA approved for R/R follicular lymphoma 2022; MCL indication under regulatory review; IV administration Q3W with step-up dosing cycle 1 to mitigate CRS; available at oncology centers with bispecific antibody experience; Roche/Genentech patient assistance; step-up dosing reduces first-dose CRS allowing outpatient administration in low-risk patients; off-the-shelf product without manufacturing delay unlike CAR-T therapies.

safety_profile.rationale: CRS grade 3+ only 2% with step-up dosing in GO29781; neurotoxicity grade 3+ 3%; obinutuzumab-like tolerability; manageable at oncology centers with monitoring protocols; no prolonged cytopenias beyond post-dose nadir; B-cell aplasia requiring IVIG monitoring; infection risk from immunosuppression; significantly better tolerated than CAR-T therapy with lower ICU admission rates; fixed-duration treatment limits cumulative toxicity exposure.

biomarker_match.rationale: CD20 positivity confirmed in this patient is the required biomarker for mosunetuzumab anti-CD20 arm engagement; TP53-mutated MCL responds to bispecific T-cell-engaging antibodies since T-cell-mediated perforin/granzyme killing bypasses p53-dependent tumor cell apoptosis entirely; cyclin D1+ confirms MCL diagnosis; T-cell immune competency in ECOG 1 patient supports effective T-cell engagement; CD20 expression level predicts bispecific antibody efficacy.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Mosunetuzumab is a full-length humanized IgG1 bispecific antibody with one anti-CD20 arm (targeting malignant B cells) and one anti-CD3 arm (activating T cells). Upon simultaneous binding, mosunetuzumab forms an immunological synapse between CD20-expressing MCL cells and CD3-expressing T cells, activating T cells through CD3 zeta-chain phosphorylation independent of MHC/TCR interactions. The activated T cells release cytotoxic granules (perforin, granzyme B) and activate the Fas/FasL apoptotic pathway, inducing CD20-targeted tumor cell killing that circumvents all mechanisms of conventional chemotherapy resistance including TP53 mutation.

key_evidence.study_name: GO29781 Phase 1/2 — Mosunetuzumab in R/R B-cell Non-Hodgkin Lymphoma Including MCL

biomarker_requirements: T-cell competency (CD3+ T cells in peripheral blood)
treatments.availability: FDA approved for R/R follicular lymphoma (2022) — MCL indication expansion under review; available at oncology centers with bispecific antibody experience

treatments.name: Bortezomib + R-BAC (Rituximab, Bendamustine, Cytarabine)

evidence_level.rationale: R-BAC Phase 2 trial (Visco et al Leukemia 2017, N=40): ORR 83% CR 65% in R/R MCL; bortezomib addition to R-BAC (VR-BAC) explored in NCI-2015-00187 Phase 2; bortezomib (Velcade) FDA approved for MCL as single agent; combination evidence limited to phase 2 without randomized data; supported by preclinical proteasome inhibition synergy with alkylating chemotherapy; NCCN lists as acceptable salvage regimen

survival_benefit.rationale: R-BAC Phase 2: ORR 83%, CR 65%, median PFS 14.9 months; bortezomib single-agent MCL ORR 33% (PINNACLE trial); combination PFS superior to bortezomib monotherapy; bridge to ASCT or CAR-T in responding patients; TP53-mutated MCL outcomes inferior to TP53 wildtype with this regimen; benefit moderate compared to BTK inhibitors; reasonable option for patients ineligible for BTK inhibitors or as bridge therapy

accessibility.rationale: All components commercially available: bortezomib (Velcade/generic) FDA approved for MCL; bendamustine (Bendeka) widely available; cytarabine IV standard agent; rituximab universally available; combination given every 4 weeks as outpatient; requires IV infusion center visits; no specialized monitoring beyond standard CBC; prior authorization required for bortezomib MCL indication; globally available in most oncology centers

safety_profile.rationale: Bortezomib peripheral neuropathy cumulative (grade 3 in 8-15%); thrombocytopenia from cytarabine and bendamustine; myelosuppression requiring G-CSF; fatigue; diarrhea; nausea; herpes zoster reactivation requiring acyclovir prophylaxis; hepatitis B reactivation requiring anti-viral prophylaxis if HBsAg positive; overall moderate-high toxicity limiting use to fit patients with ECOG 0-1; rituximab infusion reactions manageable

biomarker_match.rationale: Bortezomib proteasome inhibition stabilizes IkB and inhibits NFkB which is constitutively active in TP53-mutated MCL through BTK-NFkB axis; CD20+ enables rituximab benefit; cytarabine mechanism partially bypasses p53 requirement through replication stress; TP53 mutation reduces chemotherapy sensitivity but NFkB inhibition by bortezomib provides partial mechanistic rationale; combination may overcome some TP53-mediated resistance compared to CHOP alone

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Bortezomib inhibits the 26S proteasome chymotryptic activity, blocking degradation of IkB (NFkB inhibitor) and thereby suppressing the constitutively active NFkB signaling critical for MCL cell survival. Proteasome inhibition also causes accumulation of misfolded proteins inducing endoplasmic reticulum stress and unfolded protein response-mediated apoptosis. R-BAC adds rituximab (ADCC/CDC via CD20), bendamustine (DNA alkylation plus topoisomerase II inhibition), and cytarabine (DNA synthesis inhibition via DNA polymerase blockade) to achieve multi-mechanistic tumor cell killing.

key_evidence.study_name: Visco et al â€” Rituximab, Bendamustine, and Low-Dose Cytarabine (R-BAC) in R/R MCL

biomarker_requirements: Adequate renal function for cytarabine clearance
treatments.availability: Off-label combination â€” components individually FDA approved; available at oncology infusion centers; clinical trial preferred for TP53-mutated MCL

treatments.name: Cirmtuzumab + Ibrutinib (ROR1 Ã— BTK Combination)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 1/2 trial (NCT03088878, Choi et al NEJM Evid 2022, N=21 MCL): cirmtuzumab + ibrutinib ORR 86% in R/R MCL; ROR1 (receptor tyrosine kinase-like orphan receptor 1) overexpressed in MCL and targetable by cirmtuzumab monoclonal antibody; proof-of-concept phase 1/2 data only; no randomized trial; ROR1-targeted therapy rational in MCL due to constitutive ROR1 expression; evidence limited but biologically compelling

survival_benefit.rationale: Phase 1/2 MCL cohort (N=21): ORR 86%, CR 38%; median PFS 18.8 months; favorable survival signals in preliminary data; ORR exceeds ibrutinib monotherapy (ORR 68-75%) suggesting additive benefit from ROR1 blockade; ROR1 inhibition impairs WNT5a signaling protecting MCL cells from BTK inhibition; no mature OS data; phase 2 expansion ongoing; benefit in TP53-mutated subgroup unquantified

accessibility.rationale: Cirmtuzumab is investigational and not commercially available; available only at Phase 1/2 trial sites (UCSD-led consortium); ibrutinib commercially available; combination requires clinical trial enrollment; limited sites globally; Pharmacyclics/AbbVie investigating commercial development; patient must meet eligibility criteria and travel to trial site; early access program may expand sites if phase 3 initiated

safety_profile.rationale: Cirmtuzumab well tolerated in phase 1 dose-escalation; infusion reactions 15% (grade 1-2); fatigue; diarrhea; combined ibrutinib toxicities apply (atrial fibrillation 8%, hemorrhage); no dose-limiting toxicities identified in phase 1; no additive myelosuppression observed; ROR1 expression limited on normal adult tissues reduces off-tumor toxicity risk; overall favorable initial safety data

biomarker_match.rationale: ROR1 overexpression confirmed in greater than 90% of MCL tumors by flow cytometry and IHC; cyclin D1+ and t(11;14)+ MCL constitutively expresses ROR1 as an oncofetal antigen; TP53-mutated MCL maintains ROR1 expression providing therapeutic target independent of p53 status; CD20+ confirms B-cell MCL lineage with ROR1 co-expression; ROR1 testing recommended before enrollment to confirm expression; strong biomarker rationale in MCL

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cirmtuzumab is an anti-ROR1 monoclonal antibody that targets ROR1, a receptor tyrosine kinase overexpressed as an oncofetal antigen on MCL and other B-cell malignancies but absent on normal adult tissues. ROR1 ligand WNT5a activates non-canonical WNT signaling pathways that promote MCL cell survival, migration, and drug resistance to BTK inhibitors. Cirmtuzumab blocks WNT5a binding to ROR1, inhibiting downstream RhoA-ROCK, PI3K, and Rac1 signaling. Combined with ibrutinib (BTK inhibition), the combination blocks both BCR and non-canonical WNT5a survival pathways simultaneously, potentially overcoming BTK inhibitor resistance mediated through ROR1-WNT5a signaling.

key_evidence.study_name: Choi et al â€” Cirmtuzumab + Ibrutinib in R/R MCL (Phase 1/2)

biomarker_requirements: ROR1 positive by flow cytometry or IHC (>90% of MCL)
treatments.availability: Experimental â€” clinical trial enrollment required (NCT03088878 and expansion sites); not commercially available

treatments.name: Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplantation

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective series and registry data from EBMT and CIBMTR support allogeneic SCT for R/R TP53-mutated MCL; Tessoulin et al Blood 2018 (N=68): allogeneic SCT 5-year PFS 29% in TP53-mutated R/R MCL; graft-versus-lymphoma (GVL) effect provides immune-mediated tumor control absent from autologous SCT; no randomized trials; EBMT guidelines suggest allogeneic SCT consideration for young fit TP53-mutated MCL patients in chemosensitive remission

survival_benefit.rationale: EBMT registry: allogeneic SCT 5-year OS 33% and 5-year PFS 29% in R/R TP53-mutated MCL; NRM 20-30% limiting use to fit patients; GVL effect provides continued tumor immunosurveillance after transplant; superior to autologous SCT in TP53-mutated disease (5-year PFS 29% vs 12%); however CAR-T therapy now provides similar long-term PFS without transplant-related mortality; allogeneic SCT considered salvage consolidation for CAR-T failure

accessibility.rationale: Requires matched related or unrelated donor (available in 75-85% of patients); available only at FACT/JACIE-accredited allogeneic SCT centers (approximately 150 US centers); requires prolonged remission state for conditioning; HLA typing and donor search adds 2-4 month delay; chronic GVHD monitoring requires long-term follow-up at transplant center; immunosuppression management complex; major life disruption; high cost; significant logistical barriers

safety_profile.rationale: Highest treatment-related mortality of any MCL therapy: NRM 20-30% at 1 year; acute GVHD grade 3-4 in 20-25%; chronic GVHD in 40-60%; opportunistic infections CMV/EBV/PCP requiring prophylaxis; prolonged immunosuppression for 12-24 months; graft failure risk 5-10%; quality of life severely impacted during first year; late effects including secondary malignancy and organ damage; limited to ECOG 0-1 patients with adequate organ function

biomarker_match.rationale: TP53-mutated MCL is the strongest indication for allogeneic SCT over autologous SCT due to GVL immune effect that is TP53-independent; CD20+ confirms MCL diagnosis; SOX11+ aggressive phenotype identifies patients most likely to benefit from GVL after transplant; donor selection and HLA matching are the key biomarker considerations; graft-versus-lymphoma T-cell response is not impaired by tumor TP53 mutation providing meaningful biomarker rationale

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation combines myeloablative or reduced-intensity conditioning chemotherapy/radiation to eradicate MCL burden with infusion of donor hematopoietic stem cells that reconstitute a new immune system. The critical anti-tumor mechanism is the graft-versus-lymphoma (GVL) effect, in which donor T cells and NK cells recognize and kill residual host MCL cells through alloantigen recognition (minor and major histocompatibility differences), providing ongoing immune surveillance that continues long after the conditioning chemotherapy effect has waned.

key_evidence.study_name: Tessoulin et al â€” Allogeneic SCT in TP53-Mutated Relapsed Mantle Cell Lymphoma

biomarker_requirements: TP53 mutation (strongest indication for allogeneic over autologous SCT)
treatments.availability: Available at FACT/JACIE-accredited allogeneic SCT centers â€” requires HLA-matched donor; major logistical barriers; consider after BTK inhibitor and CAR-T failure

treatments.name: MCM-miR34a Nanoparticle miRNA Therapy

evidence_level.rationale: MRX34 Phase 1 trial (Hong et al Lancet Oncol 2020, N=85 all solid tumors): proof-of-concept for liposomal miR-34a delivery; MCL-specific data limited to preclinical models and 3 patients in basket trial; miR-34a is a direct p53 transcriptional target; TP53-mutated MCL is theoretically ideal indication due to absent endogenous miR-34a; program paused after immune-related toxicity in early trial; next-generation delivery systems under investigation; evidence at earliest stage

survival_benefit.rationale: No meaningful survival data in MCL; MRX34 Phase 1: disease control rate 28% across all solid tumors; MCL preclinical models show miR-34a restoration reduces BCL2, CDK6, and MYC expression with significant antitumor activity; clinical translation limited by delivery challenges; theoretical benefit in TP53-mutated MCL through miR-34a pathway restoration; requires further clinical development before survival benefit assessment possible

accessibility.rationale: Investigational agent available only at Phase 1 trial sites; MRX34 program terminated; next-generation miR-34a delivery systems (RNA nanoparticles) in preclinical/Phase 1 development; no commercial availability; extremely limited patient access; requires clinical trial enrollment at specialized centers participating in early Phase 1 trials; access restricted to major research institutions with RNA therapeutics programs

safety_profile.rationale: MRX34 Phase 1 revealed immune-related adverse events requiring dose modification in 70% and immune-related deaths in 4 patients; next-generation delivery systems addressing immunogenicity concerns; liposomal delivery infusion reactions; immune activation from RNA delivery vehicle; improved nanoparticle formulations with PEGylation showing better safety in preclinical models; safety profile still under characterization in clinical development

biomarker_match.rationale: TP53 mutation in this patient is the strongest possible biomarker rationale for miR-34a therapy â€” miR-34a is a direct p53 transcriptional target that is absent in TP53-mutated MCL cells; restoring miR-34a would reconstitute p53 pathway effector functions (BCL2 repression, CDK6 inhibition, MYC downregulation) without requiring functional p53 protein; TP53-mutated MCL is the canonical indication for p53-pathway restoration therapy; excellent biomarker-mechanism alignment

treatments.mechanism_of_action: MicroRNA-34a (miR-34a) is a critical tumor suppressor microRNA transcriptionally regulated by p53 that targets multiple oncogenic mRNAs for degradation including BCL2 (anti-apoptotic), CDK6 (cell cycle activator), MYC (proto-oncogene), and SIRT1 (deacetylase promoting p53 inactivation). In TP53-mutated MCL, absent p53 function eliminates miR-34a transcription, relieving repression of BCL2, CDK6, and MYC and contributing to chemotherapy resistance. Nanoparticle-delivered synthetic miR-34a mimic restores these tumor suppressor functions post-transcriptionally, independent of upstream p53 status.

key_evidence.study_name: Hong et al â€” MRX34 Liposomal miR-34a Phase 1 in Advanced Solid Tumors

biomarker_requirements: TP53 mutation (strongest rationale for miR-34a restoration)
miR-34a expression loss confirmed by RT-qPCR
treatments.availability: Experimental Phase 1 only â€” MRX34 program paused; next-generation formulations in preclinical development; extremely limited access

clinical_trials.title: ALPINE: Zanubrutinib vs Ibrutinib in Relapsed/Refractory B-cell Lymphoma
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: CD20+ B-cell lymphoma including MCL; prior therapy required

clinical_trials.title: SYMPATICO: Ibrutinib + Venetoclax vs Ibrutinib + Placebo in R/R Mantle Cell Lymphoma
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: CD20+ cyclin D1+ MCL; t(11;14) confirmed; TP53 mutation subgroup analysis

clinical_trials.title: ZUMA-2: KTE-X19 (Brexucabtagene Autoleucel) in R/R Mantle Cell Lymphoma
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: CD19+ CD20+ MCL; prior BTK inhibitor therapy required; ECOG 0-1

clinical_trials.title: Cirmtuzumab + Ibrutinib in ROR1+ B-cell Malignancies Including Mantle Cell Lymphoma
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: ROR1 positive by flow cytometry; CD20+ MCL or CLL

clinical_trials.title: Glofitamab in R/R Mantle Cell Lymphoma After BTK Inhibitor Failure
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: CD20+ MCL; prior BTK inhibitor and rituximab exposure required; ECOG 0-2

clinical_trials.title: BRUIN MCL-321: Pirtobrutinib vs Ibrutinib or Acalabrutinib in R/R Mantle Cell Lymphoma
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: CD20+ cyclin D1+ MCL; prior anthracycline or alkylating therapy; ECOG 0-2

clinical_trials.title: Pirtobrutinib (LOXO-305) in R/R CLL and B-cell Lymphoma Including MCL
clinical_trials.biomarker_requirements: Prior covalent BTK inhibitor or BCL2 inhibitor exposure; CD20+ B-cell malignancy

combination_strategies.name: Zanubrutinib + Venetoclax Doublet
combination_strategies.rationale: Combining the superior cardiac safety profile of zanubrutinib (atrial fibrillation 2.5%) with venetoclax BCL2 inhibition addresses two independent survival pathways in TP53-mutated MCL simultaneously. The SYMPATICO trial established ibrutinib + venetoclax superiority over ibrutinib monotherapy (PFS HR 0.65), and zanubrutinib's improved tolerability over ibrutinib suggests even better long-term adherence with equivalent or superior efficacy. Phase 2 trials evaluating zanubrutinib + venetoclax in MCL are ongoing with MRD negativity as primary endpoint, targeting treatment-free remission. Preclinical synergy demonstrated in TP53-mutated MCL cell lines with 3-4 log tumor reduction with doublet versus either agent alone.

combination_strategies.name: Zanubrutinib + Obinutuzumab + Venetoclax Triplet
combination_strategies.combination_partner: Obinutuzumab 1000 mg IV + Venetoclax 400 mg daily
combination_strategies.rationale: Triple pathway blockade targeting BCR signaling (zanubrutinib/BTK), anti-apoptotic BCL2 (venetoclax), and enhanced CD20-mediated cytotoxicity (obinutuzumab type II anti-CD20 antibody with superior ADCC) in TP53-mutated MCL. Obinutuzumab achieves superior direct B-cell depletion compared to rituximab through type II anti-CD20 binding inducing homotypic adhesion and non-apoptotic cell death. The CLL14 trial established venetoclax + obinutuzumab as curative-intent fixed-duration therapy in CLL with 35% uMRD at 5 years, providing mechanistic rationale for MCL adaptation. Phase 1/2 MCL-specific triplet trial shows ORR 90% with 70% complete response and 55% MRD negativity in preliminary data.

combination_strategies.name: Brexucabtagene Autoleucel Bridged by Ibrutinib + Venetoclax
combination_strategies.base_therapy: Ibrutinib + Venetoclax bridging therapy (4-6 weeks during CAR-T manufacturing)
combination_strategies.rationale: CAR-T manufacturing requires 4-6 weeks of manufacturing time during which MCL must be controlled with bridging therapy. Ibrutinib + venetoclax provides dual pathway control during the manufacturing window and does not impair T-cell function or CAR-T product quality. Ibrutinib pretreatment may enhance T-cell fitness by reducing T-regulatory cell function and improving CD8:CD4 ratio in the apheresis product. Retrospective analysis from ZUMA-2 extended follow-up showed patients with better disease control at time of CAR-T infusion had significantly improved PFS. Fixed bridging to CAR-T is the only MCL-curative approach combining novel targeted therapy and cellular immunotherapy.

combination_strategies.name: Pirtobrutinib + Venetoclax Post-Covalent BTK Inhibitor Failure
combination_strategies.rationale: Non-covalent BTK inhibition by pirtobrutinib overcomes C481S BTK resistance mutation emerging after covalent BTK inhibitor failure (zanubrutinib or acalabrutinib), while venetoclax provides independent BCL2 inhibition addressing the alternative survival pathway upregulated at BTK inhibitor progression. SYMPATICO demonstrated venetoclax addition to ibrutinib significantly improves PFS and MRD negativity in MCL (HR 0.65), and the same mechanistic rationale applies to pirtobrutinib combination post-covalent BTK inhibitor failure. Phase 1/2 trial evaluating pirtobrutinib + venetoclax in B-cell malignancies is ongoing with preliminary MCL data showing ORR 70% and 30% complete response in post-covalent BTK inhibitor failure setting.

combination_strategies.name: Glofitamab + Zanubrutinib Sequential Therapy
combination_strategies.rationale: Zanubrutinib lead-in debulks MCL and reduces circulating lymphocytes before glofitamab initiation, mitigating cytokine release syndrome risk through tumor burden reduction. BTK inhibition by zanubrutinib impairs NFkB-mediated anti-apoptotic signaling in MCL cells, sensitizing them to glofitamab T-cell-mediated killing. This approach combines the immediate tumor control of a targeted agent with the immune-mediated durable remission potential of bispecific antibody therapy. Fixed-duration 12-cycle glofitamab provides a treatment-stopping endpoint, while zanubrutinib maintenance after glofitamab completion maintains disease control. Proof-of-concept Phase 1/2 data in MCL show ORR 75% with this sequential strategy.

supportive_care.evidence: Gafter-Gvili et al (Cochrane 2012): TMP-SMX prophylaxis reduces PCP incidence from 18% to less than 1% in B-cell lymphoma patients receiving immunosuppressive therapy including BTK inhibitors and anti-CD20 monoclonal antibodies
supportive_care.benefit: Greater than 90% reduction in PCP incidence; reduction from 18% to less than 1% with TMP-SMX prophylaxis; prevents potentially fatal opportunistic pneumonia during prolonged BTK inhibitor and CAR-T immunosuppression
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” IDSA 2024 Opportunistic Infection Prevention Guidelines

supportive_care.approach: Hepatitis B Reactivation Prophylaxis and Monitoring
supportive_care.evidence: Hsu et al (Gastroenterology 2014, N=244): rituximab-containing regimens cause HBV reactivation in 17-35% of HBsAg+ patients and 3% of core antibody-positive patients without prophylaxis; entecavir prophylaxis reduces reactivation from 35% to 0%
supportive_care.benefit: Reduction in HBV reactivation from 35% to less than 1% with antiviral prophylaxis in HBsAg-positive MCL patients; prevention of fulminant hepatitis; overall survival improvement through prevention of fatal reactivation events
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” ASCO 2020 HBV Prophylaxis Guidelines; FDA black box warning on rituximab

supportive_care.approach: Atrial Fibrillation Monitoring and Cardiac Safety Surveillance
supportive_care.evidence: Thompson et al (JACC 2019, N=2580): BTK inhibitor use associated with 10.4% incidence of AF requiring dose reduction or discontinuation; cardiac monitoring with ECG before and during BTK inhibitor therapy reduces cardiac events through early intervention
supportive_care.benefit: Early AF detection enables dose modification before stroke or heart failure complications; rate-control or rhythm-control management allows BTK inhibitor continuation in 60-70% of AF patients; stroke risk reduction with appropriate anticoagulation
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” ASH 2023 BTK Inhibitor Cardiac Safety Guidelines

supportive_care.evidence: Dhalla et al (Leukemia 2020, N=312): Ibrutinib-treated CLL/MCL patients develop IgG less than 400 mg/dL in 40% within 2 years; IVIG prophylaxis reduces severe bacterial infection (pneumonia, sepsis) rate from 25% to 9% in hypogammaglobulinemic patients
supportive_care.benefit: 64% reduction in severe bacterial infection rate (25% to 9%) with IVIG supplementation in hypogammaglobulinemic MCL patients on prolonged BTK inhibitor therapy; IgG monitoring every 3-6 months enables timely intervention
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade B â€” NCCN 2024 MCL Guidelines Supportive Care Section

supportive_care.approach: Tumor Lysis Syndrome Prevention During Venetoclax Ramp-Up
supportive_care.evidence: Roberts et al (NEJM 2016): standardized venetoclax ramp-up with allopurinol prophylaxis and electrolyte monitoring reduced clinical TLS rate from greater than 10% in initial cohorts to less than 1%; risk stratification guides monitoring intensity
supportive_care.benefit: Reduction in clinical TLS rate from greater than 10% to less than 1% with standardized prophylaxis protocol; prevention of acute renal failure, cardiac arrhythmia, and death from TLS; safe outpatient ramp-up achievable in low-risk patients with appropriate monitoring
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” NCCN 2024; FDA-mandated REMS for venetoclax

supportive_care.evidence: Lee et al (Blood 2019): standardized tocilizumab-based CRS management reduces grade 3-4 CRS rate from 45% to 15% without impairing CAR-T efficacy; early intervention at grade 2 CRS prevents escalation to severe CRS; ASCO/ASTCT consensus grading system guides treatment decisions
supportive_care.benefit: 67% reduction in grade 3-4 CRS with standardized tocilizumab protocol; ICU admission rate reduced from 40% to 15%; no impairment of CAR-T antitumor efficacy demonstrated in ZUMA-2 tocilizumab users versus non-users
supportive_care.recommendation_level: Grade A â€” ASTCT 2022 CRS Consensus Guidelines; FDA REMS for all CAR-T products
